title: Nanoparticle manipulation with a carbon fiber tip in an electron microscope for $μ$-SQUID magnetometry
authors: Umesh Chandra Thuwal, Sumanta Maity, Clemens B. Winkelmann, Hervé Courtois, Anjan Kumar Gupta
abstract: We report a carbon-fiber-tip based nanomanipulation system integrated into a scanning electron microscope for individual nanoparticle (NP) manipulation on a surface. Electrochemically etched amorphous carbon fiber tips with excellent mechanical rigidity and sub-100 nm apex radii effectively reduce the van der Waals adhesion and enable reliable positioning of about 100 nm size NPs with about 100 nm precision. This system combines a piezoelectric bimorph for vertical tip motion, a four-quadrant piezo-tube for two-dimensional fine tip control and a two-dimensional piezoelectric walker for coarse lateral translation. Using this setup, we successfully position single Fe$_3$O$_4$ magnetic NPs on micron sized superconducting quantum interference devices for optimal magnetic coupling between them and probe a NP's magnetism.
summary: This work reports the development and application of a novel carbon-fiber-tip nanomanipulation system integrated into an SEM. The system achieves sub-100 nm precision to position magnetic nanoparticles ($	ext{Fe}_3	ext{O}_4$) onto $\mu$-SQUID arrays. This technique successfully enables the high-resolution, single-particle measurement of magnetic properties, advancing nano-magnetic characterization for quantum sensing applications.
main_problem: The primary goal was to develop a precise nanomanipulation system to position individual magnetic nanoparticles (NPs) near highly sensitive superconducting quantum interference devices ($\mu$-SQUIDs) for magnetic characterization.
main_result: The system successfully positioned $	ext{Fe}_3	ext{O}_4$ NPs onto $\mu$-SQUIDs, allowing for the measurement of anisotropic magnetic hysteresis loops from single NPs.
method: The authors utilized a custom carbon-fiber-tip based nanomanipulator integrated into an SEM, employing piezoelectric actuators for sub-100 nm positioning precision.
model_or_system: The platform combines a carbon-fiber tip, which is etched via electrochemical methods, with a multi-stage piezoelectric positioning system (bimorph, piezo-tube, walker). The sample consists of a silicon chip containing an array of $\mu$-SQUIDs.
key_observables: Anisotropic magnetic hysteresis loops ($	ext{B}_f-	ext{H}$), NP placement precision ($\sim 100 	ext{ nm}$), and SQUID response periodicity ($\Phi_0$).
important_parameters: Optimal tip apex radius ($\lesssim 100 	ext{ nm}$), initial etching current ($I_{	ext{in}}$) and resulting breakage time ($t_b$).
main_assumptions: The van der Waals force calculation assumes a simple sphere–sphere contact model, and the mechanical fragility of ultra-sharp tips is noted as a limitation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 details the relationship between tip breakage time and etching current. Figure 2 shows the schematic of the nano-manipulator. Figure 3 illustrates the successful placement of an NP onto a $\mu$-SQUID, and Figure 4 displays the measured magnetic hysteresis loops.
paper_structure: The paper details the development of the nanomanipulator system, characterizes the tip fabrication process, demonstrates the precise positioning of magnetic NPs onto the SQUID array, and finally presents the magnetic characterization results.
why_interesting: While the core physics is classical magnetism and condensed matter characterization, the extreme precision required for manipulating quantum-scale objects near quantum sensors (SQUIDs) touches upon the interface between classical mechanics and quantum measurement, which is relevant to quantum sensing applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Breaking time tb variation with initial current Iin and an inverse-square ﬁtting as given by Eq. 1. (b) and (c) show the tips etched at Iin = 272 and 236 µA, respectively. (d) Tip etched at Iin = 38 µA with tb = 380 s, yielding a well-tapered apex of ≲100 nm suitable for manipulation. The inset shows a larger-scale view. (e) Tip etched at Iin = 39 µA with tb = 630 s produces an ultrasharp, but fragile, tip of apex radius close to 35 nm.

Figure 2 shows the nanomanipulator system, which can be divided into two parts, namely, the tip holder assembly and the 2D walker which are rigidly ﬁxed on an aluminum platform. Further zoomed-in pictures of the nanomanipulator are given in the Suppl-Info [39]. The tip holder assembly consists of a spring-loaded stage, a piezoelectric tube (PE tube) and a piezoelectric Z- bimorph. The Z-bimorph is used for ﬁne tip motion in the Z-direction with about 10 nm precision using a DC-voltage and this motion is limited to about 100 µm. The bimorph is ﬁxed on a PE tube [40] that has its outer electrode segmented into four quadrants and a common inner electrode. This tube provides ﬁne movement, in...

Figure 2: (a) Simpliﬁed schematic of the nano-manipulator system with following components: sample (1), 2D walker platform (2), SS frame of 2D walker (3), PE bimorph actuators of 2D walker (4), tungsten wire with the carbon ﬁber tip (5), PE bimorph for z-motion of tip (6), PE tube (7), brass rod (8), spring loaded stage with setscrew (9) and aluminum platform (10). (b) Complete nano-manipulation assembly mounted inside the SEM with connecting wires (11) and SEM stage (12). In (b) one can make out the array of nine µ-SQUIDs, on the sample (1), with each SQUID having four contact pads.

Figure 3: Nano-particle manipulation using a sharp tip with an apex radius less than 100 nm. (a) The particle, approximately 150 nm in size, is initially located about 2 µm away from the µ-SQUID. (b) The particle is successfully dragged using the tip and brought closer to the µ-SQUID, at a distance of about 500 nm. (c) The NP is successfully placed in the middle of the top right inner edge of the µ-SQUID loop. (d) A closeup view of the µ-SQUID with the NP in the ﬁnal state. The outer edge of the SQUID loop has been marked by red discontinuous line. The NP is slightly shifted by about 100 nm from its previous position (marked by the cross) of image (c) to its current position. Note that the...

Figure 3 shows the SEM images taken during the manipulation of a NP where an Fe3O4 NP is dragged by several microns and positioned on the µ-SQUID. In Fig. 3(a), the labeled NP, together with two other NPs, is seen at about 2 µm distance from the µ-SQUID edge. In this image, One can also see two NPs adhering to the side of the tip. This is found to happen occasionally but such attachment is typically seen to occur away from the apex due a larger contact area. However, the tip apex remains sharp and clean, permitting successful manip- ulation of a NP. The µ-SQUID loop with two parallel constrictions is marked in Fig. 3(d).
